bullets.text: Net premiums written surged 36% to $4,665.1 million from $3,441.7 million in 1996, with Core business NPW up 33% to $4,467.4 million; total auto policyholders grew by 575,000 to 2.5 million.

Progressive posted its 25th underwriting profit in 31 years with a 6.6% margin; net income rose 28% to $400.0 million ($5.31/share); ROE was 20.9%; total shareholder return in 1997 was 78.4%, ranking 28th in the S&P 500.

Progressive introduced credit-based rating in 31 states representing 80% of core written premiums; countrywide auto rates were lowered an average of 0.9%, producing more accurate pricing and lower rates for most consumers.

Progressive became the first auto insurer to conduct direct Internet sales and accept online payments in 1997; the website won multiple awards including ComputerWorld's Premier 100 and WebMaster's top 50.

Standard and preferred risks grew to 20-25% of 1997 core premium volume; independent agencies writing Progressive standard and preferred products more than doubled, with agent-produced standard/preferred new policies up 126%.

Underwriting expense ratio rose slightly to 22.5% from 21.6% in 1996 due to infrastructure expansion for hyper-growth and Gainsharing payout at 14.4% of compensation, which was 0.5 points above target.

Progressive improved timeliness of Immediate Response claims despite handling 16 million phone calls; deployed wireless Claims Workbench for field representatives and installed new phone switches in claim branches.

Progressive expanded from 37 to 54 business units; acquired Midland Financial Group for ~$50 million, adding $84.1 million in 1997 revenues; maintained A-rated senior debt with disciplined capital structure.

Progressive expanded advertising to 40+ media markets in 19 states; multiple distribution channels (agents, 1-800 AUTO PRO, Internet) all supported with consumer brand advertising featuring situation-based commercials.

The property-casualty industry's net premiums written to surplus ratio of 0.9:1—the lowest in 60 years—indicated excess capitalization and risk of competitors accepting lower returns, increasing competitive pressure.
